Cartilaginous joints can be further divided into two categories—primary and secondary—which differ mainly in the type of cartilage involved.

Primary cartilaginous joints, also known as synchondroses, consist entirely of hyaline cartilage connecting two bones. An example would be the epiphyseal plate found in long bones, where growth occurs through chondrocyte activity. On the other hand, secondary cartilaginous joints, or symphyses, contain both hyaline and fibrocartilage; they provide more flexibility while still maintaining structural integrity. These often occur at sites requiring shock absorption but not necessarily growth potential.
